P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322-5662 
Kirk Alan Pessner 
Dobbs & Nielsen 
Administration 
322-5660 
Suite 2500, The Alcoa Building 
One Maritime Plaza 
San Francisco, CA 
94111 
Dear Kirk: 
Executive/legal 
322-5901 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
March 23, 1983 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
This is in reply to your letter dated December 22, 1982, regarding 
the purchase of tickets to the gubernatorial inaugural events. 
I 
apologize for the inordinate delay in providing written 
confirmation of the advice I provided to you by telephone. 
We were informed by the Inaugural Committee that the price of the 
tickets for the inaugural events covered the actual costs of 
putting on the events and, if there were any funds left over after 
paying the costs, the surplus funds would be donated to the State 
to be used for restoration of the Governor's Mansion. 
Bec~use no part of the purchase price of the tickets was used for 
political purposes or for the purpose of making gifts to public 
officials, the purchase of the tickets is neither a Hcontribution n 
(Gov. Code Section 82015) nor a "gift" (Gov. Code Section 82028), 
as those terms are defined in the Political Reform Act, and those 
persons purchasing tickets have no disclosure obligation under the 
Act. 
Please contact me if you have any further questions. 
Thank you for 
your patience. 
Sincerely, 
Jeanne Pritchard 
Political Reform Consultant

Fair Political Practices 
Commission 
1100 'K' Street 
Sacramento, CA 
94814 
SUBJECT: 
Purchase of Tickets to 
Inaugural Events 
Dear Jeannie: 
This letter will request that you confirm for us in 
writing our telephone conversation of today in which you 
advised that the purchase of tickets for the 1983 Inaugural 
Events or the contribution of money to defray the costs of 
the Inauguration are not considered a contribution under 
Government Code §820l5 nor reportable as such pursuant to 
Government Code §84000 et seq. 
Additionally, please confirm that the purchase of 
tickets to the Inaugural Event or contributions to defray 
the cost of the Inauguration are in no way considered to 
be a reportable lobbying activity expense which would be 
reported by either Government Code §-&6lJ1Q or 86108 filers.

Thank you for your attentionito this matter.

sections.question: This letter will request that you confirm for us in writing our telephone conversation of today in which you advised that the purchase of tickets for the 1983 Inaugural Events or the contribution of money to defray the costs of the Inauguration are not considered a contribution under Government Code §82015 nor reportable as such pursuant to Government Code §84000 et seq. Additionally, please confirm that the purchase of tickets to the Inaugural Event or contributions to defray the cost of the Inauguration are in no way considered to be a reportable lobbying activity expense which would be reported by either Government Code §86104 or 86108 filers.
sections.conclusion: Because no part of the purchase price of the tickets was used for political purposes or for the purpose of making gifts to public officials, the purchase of the tickets is neither a "contribution" (Gov. Code Section 82015) nor a "gift" (Gov. Code Section 82028), as those terms are defined in the Political Reform Act, and those persons purchasing tickets have no disclosure obligation under the Act.
sections.question_synthetic: Are purchases of tickets to gubernatorial inaugural events considered political contributions requiring disclosure under the Political Reform Act, and are such purchases reportable as lobbying activity expenses?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that ticket purchases for inaugural events are not contributions or gifts under the Political Reform Act because the ticket prices cover only actual event costs with any surplus donated to the State, and therefore no disclosure obligation exists.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains incoming request letter dated December 22, 1982 and FPPC response dated March 23, 1983. Response letter signed by Jeanne Pritchard, Political Reform Consultant. Some OCR artifacts present in scanned documents.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: This letter will request that you confirm for us in writing our telephone conversation of today in which you advised that the purchase of tickets for the 1983 Inaugural Events or the contribution of money to defray the costs of the Inauguration are not considered a contribution under Government Code §82015 nor reportable as such pursuant to Government Code §84000 et seq. Additionally, please confirm that the purchase of tickets to the Inaugural Event or contributions to defray the cost of the Inauguration are in no way considered to be a reportable lobbying activity expense which would be reported by either Government Code §86104 or 86108 filers.

CONCLUSION: Because no part of the purchase price of the tickets was used for political purposes or for the purpose of making gifts to public officials, the purchase of the tickets is neither a "contribution" (Gov. Code Section 82015) nor a "gift" (Gov. Code Section 82028), as those terms are defined in the Political Reform Act, and those persons purchasing tickets have no disclosure obligation under the Act.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to whether purchases of tickets to 1983 gubernatorial inaugural events constitute reportable contributions. The FPPC confirmed that ticket purchases are not contributions, gifts, or reportable lobbying expenses under the Political Reform Act.
